key_insight: Natural fractures redirect hydraulic fractures by locally redistributing stress, and the sign of the induced shear stress/strain determines whether the fracture is repelled or attracted.
engineering_relevance: Understanding local stress redistribution around pre-existing fractures can improve prediction and control of hydraulic fracture paths in subsurface stimulation and storage.
method: Coupled laboratory hydraulic-fracturing tests on intact and pre-fractured PMMA specimens under plane-strain conditions
Digital image correlation to measure full-field displacement and strain during loading and injection
Poroelastic extended finite element simulations to reproduce fracture curvature and interaction modes
evidence_signal: The study combines controlled experiments and numerical reproduction of fracture trajectories, showing that intact specimens propagate in pure Mode I while pre-fractured specimens require mixed-mode (Mode I-II) criteria.
watch_item: Results are based on PMMA analog specimens and idealized boundary conditions, so field-scale heterogeneity and fluid-rock complexity may alter fracture interaction behavior.
paper_title: Local Stress Redistribution Controls Interactions between Hydraulic Fractures and Pre-existing Fractures
